content.text: Building a Safe, Parallel WooCommerce Crawler in Go with Colly

content.html: Web crawling often starts simple: fetch a listing page, extract some fields, and move on. The complexity appears when you need to enrich data by visiting detail pages, do it in parallel, and still keep your code race-free, testable, and maintainable.
This article walks through the evolution of a Go-based crawler built with Colly, focusing on the architectural decisions that make it safe and production-ready.

content.alt: Building a Safe, Parallel WooCommerce Crawler in Go with Colly

content.html: The goal was to crawl a WooCommerce website and extract:
The challenge was to:

items: Separate listing crawling from detail crawling

content.html: The first change was extending the product model to support enrichment from product pages:

content.code:
type Product struct {
    Title string
    Price string
    Link string
    Image string
    Description string
}

content.html: This makes it explicit that descriptions are not available at listing time and must be fetched later.
The crawler is implemented as a service with a clear lifecycle:
Key points:
The crawler runs in three deterministic phases:
This separation ensures:

items: Detail crawling can be parallelized independently
Cleanup runs only after all data is available

content.html: The listing crawler only extracts what is available on category pages:
No concurrency is needed here. The result is a stable slice of products that becomes the input for the next phase.
This is where most crawlers break.
Colly already provides a safe async engine:
Concurrency is controlled via LimitRule:
No goroutines around Visit() are used — Colly handles scheduling internally.
Each request carries the index of the product it belongs to:
This allows mapping a product page back to the correct slice element without lookups or maps.
Parallel callbacks mean concurrent writes. This line is not safe without protection:
The fix is explicit synchronization:
This guarantees:
The cleanup step converts scraped strings into normalized data:

content.code:
cleaned = append(cleaned, Product{
    Title:       title,
    Price:       strconv.Itoa(priceInt),
    Link:        p.Link,
    Image:       p.Image,
    Description: p.Description,
})

content.html: This separation ensures that scraping concerns never leak into business logic.
An integration test validates the full pipeline:
Key benefits:
The crawler is now:
This pattern scales cleanly to thousands of products and is suitable for production use.
